Barry Schwartz:
Barry Schwartz

Barry Schwartz is an American psychologist, Emeritus Professor of Psychology at Swarthmore College, and Visiting Professor of Management at the Haas School of Business at UC Berkeley. He is best known for his book "The Paradox of Choice: Why More Is Less" (2004), which challenges a central premise of Western consumer culture—that more choices lead to greater freedom and satisfaction. Schwartz argues instead that excessive choice leads to decision paralysis, increased anxiety, and paradoxically less satisfaction with our choices. His research distinguishes between two types of decision-makers: maximizers (who seek the absolute best option) and satisficers (who aim for "good enough" choices). The book has become foundational in understanding consumer behavior, decision psychology, and wellbeing, demonstrating that reducing choices can alleviate anxiety and improve decision quality. His influential TED Talk on the paradox of choice has reached millions of viewers, and his work has profoundly influenced how businesses, designers, and policymakers approach choice architecture and consumer experience.

In 1934, publishing executive Leon Shimkin of Simon & Schuster attended one of Carnegie's 14-week courses on human relations and public speaking and persuaded Carnegie to publish a stenographer's notes from the course. Shankar Vedantam is a science correspondent, journalist, and author best known as the host of NPR's Hidden Brain podcast and radio show, which explores the unconscious patterns that drive human behavior and has become one of the most downloaded podcasts in the world with more than 3 million weekly listeners. He is the author of The Hidden Brain: How Our Unconscious Minds Elect Presidents, Control Markets, Wage Wars, and Save Our Lives (2010) and Useful Delusions: The Power and Paradox of the Self-Deceiving Brain (2021, with Bill Mesler), both of which examine how cognitive biases and unconscious processes shape individual and collective decisions in ways we rarely recognize. Vedantam spent more than a decade as a national correspondent for The Washington Post, covering social science research and translating complex behavioral science findings into accessible, story-driven journalism. His work has been recognized with numerous awards, and his ability to connect cutting-edge research in psychology, neuroscience, and sociology to real-world events has made Hidden Brain essential listening for policymakers, educators, and business leaders. He is widely credited with helping mainstream behavioral science for general audiences alongside writers like Malcolm Gladwell and Daniel Kahneman.

Source: NPR, Amazon Author Page
Sheryl Sandberg:
Sheryl Sandberg

Sheryl Sandberg is an American technology executive, philanthropist, and writer who served as Chief Operating Officer of Meta Platforms from 2008 to 2022. She holds a BA summa cum laude from Harvard University and an MBA with highest distinction from Harvard Business School. Before Meta, she was Vice President of Global Online Sales and Operations at Google, Chief of Staff for the U.S. Treasury Department under President Clinton, a management consultant with McKinsey & Company, and an economist with the World Bank. Her book "Lean In: Women, Work, and the Will to Lead" (2013), co-authored with Nell Scovell, became an international phenomenon and bestseller that sold over one million copies, sparking a global conversation about women in leadership and workplace equality. She later co-authored "Option B" (2017) with Adam Grant, which addresses building resilience in the face of adversity. Sandberg is the founder of LeanIn.Org and is recognized as a major voice in feminist business leadership and organizational culture.

Simon Sinek:
Simon Sinek

Simon Sinek is an author and leadership expert best known for his groundbreaking book "Start with Why: How Great Leaders Inspire Everyone to Take Action" (2009), which became a New York Times and Wall Street Journal bestseller. He is the creator of the Golden Circle framework, a powerful communication model based on three concentric circles representing "Why" (purpose), "How" (processes and values), and "What" (products or services). Sinek's framework draws on neuroscience, connecting the "What" to the neocortex responsible for rational thought and the "How" and "Why" to the limbic brain, which controls emotions, behavior, and decision-making. His central insight—"People don't buy what you do; they buy why you do it"—has transformed how businesses, nonprofits, and leaders communicate their vision and attract loyal followers. His work has had profound impact on organizational culture, leadership development, and strategic communication worldwide.

Stephen Hawking:
Stephen Hawking

Stephen William Hawking (1942-2018) was an English theoretical physicist, cosmologist, and author who served as the Lucasian Professor of Mathematics at the University of Cambridge from 1979 to 2009. His theoretical prediction that black holes emit radiation — now known as Hawking radiation — fundamentally changed the field of theoretical physics and combined quantum field theory with general relativity into a single framework. His popular science book A Brief History of Time remained on the British Sunday Times bestsellers list for a record-breaking 237 weeks and sold more than 10 million copies worldwide, making complex cosmology accessible to general audiences for the first time. Despite being diagnosed with a slow-progressing form of motor neurone disease (ALS) at age 21 — a condition that ultimately left him almost entirely paralyzed and speaking through a voice synthesizer — Hawking continued groundbreaking research for more than five decades. He also co-authored The Grand Design (2010) with Leonard Mlodinow, in which he argued that a creator was not necessary to explain the origins of the universe, sparking worldwide debate.

Source: Stephen Hawking Foundation, Wikipedia

Valerie Thomas:
Valerie Thomas

Valerie Thomas is an American physicist and inventor born on February 8, 1943, in Baltimore, Maryland. She earned her degree in physics with highest honors from Morgan State University in 1964, where she was one of only two women majoring in physics. Beginning her career at NASA in 1964 as a data analyst, Thomas helped develop image-processing systems for NASA's Landsat program, the longest-running satellite program for monitoring Earth's surface. She served as leader of the Large Area Crop Inventory Experiment, researching ways to use satellite imaging to monitor agricultural yields globally. In 1976, Thomas invented the Illusion Transmitter after being intrigued by 3D optical illusions, and received a patent for this groundbreaking device in 1980, which creates the appearance of three-dimensional images using light and optical reflection technology. Before retiring from NASA in 1995 as associate chief of the Space Science Data Operations Office, she became a mentor to young people, particularly African Americans and girls, encouraging them to pursue science education. In 2018, she was inducted into the National Inventors Hall of Fame for her pioneering contributions to satellite communications and remote sensing.

William Bridges:
William Bridges

William Bridges is an organizational consultant and author best known for developing the Bridges Transition Model, a foundational framework for understanding and managing organizational change that has been used by leaders and management consultants for over 30 years. His seminal 1991 book "Managing Transitions: Making the Most of Change" introduced the concept of distinguishing between "change" (external events) and "transition" (the internal psychological process people go through). The model identifies three critical stages: Ending (what is being left behind), the Neutral Zone (the in-between period of uncertainty), and New Beginning (when stability and clarity return and people embrace the new reality). His framework has become foundational to change management practice because it recognizes that successful organizational transformation requires attending to the psychological and emotional dimensions of change, not just the logistical aspects. Bridges' work has profoundly influenced how leaders approach organizational transitions and manage the human side of change.
